title: Fledgling

jokes.text: Whoever you are, go away!
jokes.setup: Elaine confidently claims Craig's reclusive reputation is exaggerated
jokes.punchline: Craig immediately proves her wrong with an aggressive dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect character introduction through contradiction. Elaine's confidence is immediately deflated by Craig's hostile response, establishing his agoraphobic nature while creating comedy through misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I don't think your reputation for being reclusive is really fair. You're actually quite sociable.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Get out of my apartment.

jokes.text: I have a... an extremely vicious dog in here.
jokes.setup: Craig needs an excuse not to open the door
jokes.punchline: His hesitant delivery suggests he's making up a weak excuse on the spot
jokes.explanation: Craig's nervous delivery and obvious fabrication creates humor through his transparent attempt at deception.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I... I can't open the door right now. I'm... I'm in the middle of something very important. Very important. I can't be disturbed.

jokes.text: That was fun. Thank you very much. Let's go.
jokes.setup: Craig rudely shouts at them to go away
jokes.punchline: Alex sarcastically thanks Craig for the 'fun' experience
jokes.explanation: Classic Alex sarcasm - dry and understated reaction to the awkward situation.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Get out of here! Go away!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, thank you, Craig. That was a real fun experience.

jokes.text: Go to your place!
jokes.setup: Craig claimed to have a vicious dog
jokes.punchline: He's talking to himself, revealing there is no dog
jokes.explanation: Brilliant payoff to the dog setup. The economy of revealing Craig IS the 'dog' while showing his self-isolation creates multiple layers of humor and pathos.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got a real vicious dog at home. Meanest thing you ever saw.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: One time a guy tried to break in, and that dog just tore him to shreds.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Another time, a cat came in the yard, and that dog...

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Who am I kidding? I don't have a dog.

jokes.text: Craig, go to your spot. I see.
jokes.setup: Earlier dog commands revealed to be self-directed
jokes.punchline: Craig continues the pattern, Elaine realizes the truth
jokes.explanation: Strong callback that deepens the character while creating awkward recognition moment for Elaine. The cringe comes from witnessing Craig's coping mechanism.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Sit!

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Good boy.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Craig, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Stay!

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: That's a good Craig.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh my God. You've been talking to yourself this whole time, haven't you?

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Down boy!

jokes.text: You've painted this whole apartment in bits and pieces. I recognize this chair... You painted this table.
jokes.setup: Alex examines Craig's apartment
jokes.punchline: Realizes Craig has been painting his own mundane surroundings obsessively
jokes.explanation: Good character revelation showing Craig's limited world through his art. The humor comes from recognizing the absurdity of painting the same furniture repeatedly.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Craig, what is all this?

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: My paintings.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: But these are... these are just pictures of your apartment. Your couch, your window, your kitchen table...

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I paint what I know.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Craig, you've painted your radiator forty-seven times. Forty-seven!

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Each one captures a different mood.

jokes.text: I'll put the water on, and you go down and get the coffee, okay?
jokes.setup: Craig offers to take them out for coffee
jokes.punchline: His 'solution' requires them to leave while he stays put
jokes.explanation: Perfect character-specific solution that reveals Craig's mindset. He wants to be hospitable but can't leave, so he creates this logical-to-him workaround.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I'll take you all out for coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: You go ahead, I'll catch up with you later.

jokes.text: It means you're afraid of heights. No, no, that's acrophobia.
jokes.setup: Alex confidently defines agoraphobia incorrectly
jokes.punchline: Craig politely corrects him, showing Alex's ignorance
jokes.explanation: Standard misunderstanding humor. Works because it shows Alex trying to be helpful but failing, while Craig remains patient despite his condition.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Agoraphobia is the fear of agora, which is a large open marketplace. So it's the fear of open spaces and crowds.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Actually, Alex, agoraphobia is more specifically the fear of open or crowded spaces where escape might be difficult, or the fear of having a panic attack in public. It's not just about marketplaces.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Oh.

jokes.text: That's fear of England. Yeah, I have sort of just a whisper of that myself... the name Alistair Cooke gives me the shivers.
jokes.setup: Discussion of various phobias
jokes.punchline: Craig admits to multiple increasingly specific fears, ending with British TV personality
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation showing Craig's neuroses extend to absurdly specific targets. The specificity of 'Alistair Cooke' makes it memorably weird while staying true to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I'm afraid of heights, crowds, and open spaces.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Also afraid of closed spaces, spiders, and needles.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Oh, and I have this terrible fear of British television personality David Niven.

jokes.text: If you're afraid to leave your studio, what do you do when you go out? I just don't go out.
jokes.setup: Elaine asks the logical question about Craig's condition
jokes.punchline: Craig's matter-of-fact response to an obviously impossible situation
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery that highlights the circular logic of agoraphobia. The simplicity of the response makes the impossibility of his situation both funny and sad.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: How are you even alive?

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I don't know. I guess I'm just lucky.

jokes.text: That man is damn near psychotic. Uh, no offense. None taken.
jokes.setup: Alex insults Craig right in front of him
jokes.punchline: Craig's polite, unruffled acceptance of being called psychotic
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Craig's self-awareness and lack of defensiveness. The polite exchange about his mental state creates absurd normalcy.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You're psychotic.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Thank you.

jokes.text: I'm not genophobic. That means you have no fear of touching? No, it means I have no fear of sex. As long as it's right here, right now, pronto, see?
jokes.setup: Craig mentions another phobia he doesn't have
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's about sex, then adds specific conditions that make it agoraphobia-compliant
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection that starts as potentially inappropriate but ends up reinforcing his apartment-bound condition. Shows his sexuality is still limited by his phobia.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I don't have a phobia of sex.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Really? What kind of phobia do you have?

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Well, it's sex... but only in open spaces. And only when other people might see me.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's not a phobia of sex. That's agoraphobia.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: No, no. It's definitely a sex phobia. I'm fine with agoraphobia... as long as I'm alone.

jokes.text: Oh. I'm just kidding.
jokes.setup: Craig's sexual proposition hangs in the air
jokes.punchline: He immediately deflates the tension by admitting he was joking
jokes.explanation: Simple tension release that shows Craig has some social awareness and humor about his condition, though the timing creates awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I was just joking.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, that was inappropriate.

jokes.text: You got a real ark, huh?
jokes.setup: Craig mentions having 'practically two of everything'
jokes.punchline: Elaine makes Noah's ark reference about his hoarding
jokes.explanation: Decent wordplay connecting Craig's hoarding to Noah's ark. Not particularly original but fits the conversation naturally.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, with all those duplicates, you're like Noah's ark. Except instead of two of every animal, you've got two of every thing.

jokes.text: Well, if it isn't Nurse Ratched. What are you doing here? Who's watching little cuckoo's nest?
jokes.setup: Louie sees Elaine has returned
jokes.punchline: Compares her to the controlling nurse from One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie insult that's both cruel and apt. The literary reference elevates it while staying true to his character's mean-spirited observations.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Elaine! You're back! Welcome back!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, you remind me of Nurse Ratched from One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest. Just as controlling, just as cold.

jokes.text: I used to be hard of hearing, but now that you touched me, I can hear again.
jokes.setup: Louie continues mocking Elaine's 'healing' abilities
jokes.punchline: Fake miracle cure testimony in televangelist style
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the Nurse Ratched bit into faith healer territory. Shows Louie's theatrical cruelty and skepticism of Elaine's amateur therapy.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Oh sure, sure, healing powers. What are you gonna do next, walk on water?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Louie, I have a gift.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, a gift for separating people from their money. Ladies and gentlemen, I was blind, and this woman touched my head, and now I can see! Hallelujah!

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: That's not funny.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I was lame, and she rubbed my legs, and now I can walk! Thank you, Jesus! Thank you, Elaine! I'm cured!

jokes.text: She's doing it again, folks. I love my cage. I think of it as a window on the world.
jokes.setup: Louie addresses an imaginary audience about Elaine
jokes.punchline: Then makes philosophical comment about his dispatcher booth
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie moment of mock-philosophizing about his position. The irony of calling his workplace a 'window on the world' while criticizing others fits his character.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: You know, Elaine, she comes in here, she's got all these problems, all these complaints. But let me tell you something - at least she's got the guts to say what she thinks. Me? I sit in this booth all day, I don't say nothing. I just watch. I watch all of you. And you know what I've learned? Life is like being a dispatcher. Everybody's going somewhere, nobody knows where they're going, and I'm the only one who knows what's really happening.

jokes.text: Louie walking away on command as Elaine describes Alex following her
jokes.setup: Louie narrating Alex and Elaine's movements
jokes.punchline: Louie himself performs the exact behavior he's describing
jokes.explanation: Clever visual joke. Louie unconsciously demonstrates the behavior he's observing, showing his own predictability.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alex and Elaine walked into the garage, and without saying a word to each other, they immediately started making out.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I watched them for a moment, then I walked over to the filing cabinet, opened the drawer, pulled out a bottle of scotch, and took a long drink.

jokes.text: He walks to his mailbox now. Oh. Mm-hmm. On command?
jokes.setup: Louie continues his demonstration
jokes.punchline: Points out that Elaine has simply trained Craig like a pet
jokes.explanation: Strong continuation of the dog training callback that undermines Elaine's therapeutic achievements by showing them as mere conditioning.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You see? You've trained him just like a pet. Here, Craig, do a trick!

jokes.text: Just my luck. Nothing to throw away.
jokes.setup: Louie goes through motion of checking for mail to discard
jokes.punchline: Complains about not having anything to throw away, revealing his petty nature
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character moment showing his petty desire for small acts of power and meanness. The deadpan delivery sells his disappointment.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What do you mean there's no mail? How am I supposed to throw something away if there's nothing to throw away?

jokes.text: Nardo, help me. I got a phobia about getting naked.
jokes.setup: Elaine explains her therapeutic methods
jokes.punchline: Louie requests help with his 'phobia' in a sexually inappropriate way
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie sexual harassment disguised as therapy request. While character-appropriate, it's a cheap laugh that relies on his consistent creepiness.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I use a very effective therapeutic technique. I help people confront their fears directly.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, I got a real bad phobia. Maybe you could help me get over it?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Of course. What's your phobia?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I'm afraid of women. Why don't you come back to my office and help me work through it?

jokes.text: This is an expensive vintage wine that I got from one of my first fares in 1957.
jokes.setup: Louie produces an old bottle of wine, setting up what seems like a meaningful gesture
jokes.punchline: The 'expensive vintage' comes from a taxi fare, suggesting it's probably worthless
jokes.explanation: Sets up Louie's tendency to oversell ordinary things. The contradiction between 'expensive vintage' and 'taxi fare' hints at his self-deception.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I got this bottle of wine back in '62. Some guy left it in my cab. I been savin' it all these years for a special occasion.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: This is a very expensive vintage. Very rare.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: The guy who left it in my cab said it was worth a fortune.

jokes.text: This sure ain't it. I wouldn't break out a bottle of Yoo-hoo for this one.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions waiting for the right occasion
jokes.punchline: Dramatically understates how unworthy this situation is
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Louie's contempt. The Yoo-hoo reference is perfect for his character - cheap but delivered as if it's meaningful.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I've been waiting for the right occasion to wear this suit.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I guess a minor traffic violation will do.

jokes.text: We've all trusted skirts. We've all paid.
jokes.setup: Louie explains Alex's mistake in trusting Elaine
jokes.punchline: Delivers misogynistic folk wisdom in his typical blunt style
jokes.explanation: Pure Louie dialogue - crude but economical. While the sentiment is problematic, it's perfectly in character for his 1950s worldview.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You trusted a woman? What are you, crazy? Listen to me, Alex. Women are like cabs in New York. Even if they're marked 'occupied,' they'll still stop for you.

jokes.text: The man who gave me this bottle of wine was a man that women loved. His name was Errol Flynn.
jokes.setup: Louie builds up the wine's provenance with a mysterious introduction
jokes.punchline: Names a famous Hollywood swashbuckler, immediately making the story suspicious
jokes.explanation: Good setup for what's clearly going to be a tall tale. Louie's dramatic delivery of Flynn's name signals this is likely fabricated or exaggerated.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: This wine, it's got quite a history. Belonged to a very famous man once.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A real swashbuckler. A legend in his time.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Errol Flynn.

jokes.text: Better than beautiful women. Cheap women.
jokes.setup: Louie describes Flynn with two beautiful women
jokes.punchline: Corrects himself with a more crude but honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie moment - starts with romantic language then immediately undercuts it with his crude honesty. Shows his inability to maintain even fake sophistication.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Flynn was with two beautiful women.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, let me rephrase that. Flynn was with two women.

jokes.text: So, what could I do? Me and the two babes rolled him.
jokes.setup: Flynn is passed out, no doorman around, seeming like Louie will help
jokes.punchline: Reveals he robbed the unconscious movie star with the women
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark turn that's shocking but perfectly in character. The casual delivery of 'rolled him' makes it even funnier, showing Louie's complete lack of shame about criminal behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'll take care of Flynn. I'll make sure he gets upstairs safe and sound.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah, I robbed him. Look at this watch, these cufflinks, this wallet. The guy's passed out cold and there's no doorman around. What am I, a saint?

jokes.text: Then they put the knife to my throat and sold me the bottle of wine for $184.
jokes.setup: Louie and the women split Flynn's belongings
jokes.punchline: The women immediately rob Louie of all his share
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that turns Louie from criminal to victim instantly. The irony of being robbed by his partners in crime, ending up with nothing, is beautifully constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Alright, ladies, we split everything fair and square. You each get your pick.

dialogue.speaker: Woman 1
dialogue.line: I'll take this watch.

dialogue.speaker: Woman 2
dialogue.line: And I'll take that ring.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Wait, what about my share?

dialogue.speaker: Woman 1
dialogue.line: Your share? Hand it over, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Hey! That's mine!

jokes.text: What chance does an anteater like you have?
jokes.setup: Louie explains how both he and Flynn were betrayed by women
jokes.punchline: Compares Alex to an anteater in terms of attractiveness/ability with women
jokes.explanation: Brilliant insult that's both specific and devastating. 'Anteater' is an unexpected animal comparison that's funny because it's so random yet somehow apt for Alex's appearance.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, Flynn and me, we've both been betrayed by women. It's a sad thing.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: But at least Flynn's got an excuse. He looks like an anteater.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: What's Alex's excuse?

jokes.text: Should we all rest or just him?
jokes.setup: Elaine suggests they all rest while Craig recovers in the cab
jokes.punchline: Jim's literal interpretation of a figure of speech
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim Ignatowski moment showing his drug-addled literal thinking. His confusion about whether everyone needs to physically rest is both funny and character-defining.

jokes.text: Take me to Yankee Stadium.
jokes.setup: Craig is locked in a taxi cab, panicking
jokes.punchline: Gives a taxi destination as if this is a normal ride
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment where Craig's panic manifests as normal taxi passenger behavior. Shows his brain trying to process the situation through familiar patterns.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Driver! Driver, let me out! I'm locked in here!

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Wait, wait... okay, okay. Just take me to 42nd and Park. And step on it!

jokes.text: I used to pay hard-earned money to get to feel the way you feel.
jokes.setup: Jim tries to console the panicking Craig
jokes.punchline: References his drug use as paying for anxiety states
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark humor that reframes Craig's terror as something Jim paid to experience through drugs. Shows Jim's warped perspective while being oddly comforting.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Craig, calm down. You're working yourself into a state.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I can't help it, Jim! I'm freaking out!

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Listen to me. You've paid good money for anxiety states before. You can handle this one for free.

jokes.text: You do that until your hand is like a raw cutlet.
jokes.setup: Tony describing aggressive handball therapy
jokes.punchline: Grotesque but specific description of self-harm
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony line. 'Raw cutlet' is specific and gross enough to be memorable, shows his working-class directness.

jokes.text: Could I talk to the guy who's on drugs again?
jokes.setup: Craig has been subjected to various unhelpful advice
jokes.punchline: Politely requests to return to Jim, implying he was more helpful
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback showing Craig's polite desperation. The fact that the drug user was his preferred counselor is both funny and sad, perfectly delivered with understated panic.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Can I go back to Jim now?

jokes.text: Next time I'll give jump rope.
jokes.setup: Tony's handball advice clearly failed to help Craig
jokes.punchline: Suggests an even more vigorous exercise as if that was the problem
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's complete misreading of the situation. His solution to Craig's terror of physical activity is more physical activity.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know what your problem is? You're not exercising enough. You need to take up handball.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I tried handball, Tony. It didn't help.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Handball? That's nothing. You need to take up squash. Now that's exercise!

jokes.text: If they think they can do it better than me, then why aren't they up here doing it themselves?
jokes.setup: Bobby shares Peter O'Toole's advice about stage fright
jokes.punchline: The advice is about dismissing audience criticism
jokes.explanation: Standard motivational advice that seems initially helpful but sets up Craig's devastating response about what if great actors were watching.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Peter O'Toole told me the secret to getting over stage fright. He said, 'Just remember, the people in the audience—they're nobody. They don't matter. Ignore them completely.'

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I said, 'Peter, that's not getting over stage fright. That's just becoming a jerk.'

jokes.text: But what if Lawrence Olivier and Marlon Brando and Vanessa Redgrave were sitting out there?
jokes.setup: Bobby shares Peter O'Toole's advice about stage fright
jokes.punchline: Craig immediately escalates to the worst possible scenario of legendary actors watching
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Craig's tendency to imagine worst-case scenarios. His anxiety brain working perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Peter O'Toole told me the secret to stage fright is to imagine the audience in their underwear.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Yeah, well I tried that once. Then I imagined Laurence Olivier in his underwear. Then I imagined Marlon Brando in his underwear. Then I imagined Spencer Tracy in his underwear. And suddenly I'm more nervous than ever!

jokes.text: But what if Lawrence Olivier and Marlon Brando and Vanessa Redgrave were sitting out there?... what if it was an actors benefit and every great actor in the whole world was sitting out there, then what do you do?
jokes.setup: Bobby's advice about dismissing audience criticism
jokes.punchline: Craig escalates to the worst possible scenario - an audience of theater legends
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation showing Craig's anxious mind immediately jumping to worst-case scenarios. The specificity of naming theater legends makes it both funny and psychologically accurate for anxiety.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: You can't let the audience get to you. Just dismiss their criticism.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: Yeah, you're right. I mean, what's the worst that could happen? I could be performing in front of Laurence Olivier, Marlon Brando, and Tennessee Williams, and they could all walk out.

jokes.text: I don't deserve to be in the theater.
jokes.setup: Craig's devastating scenario of being judged by theater legends
jokes.punchline: Bobby immediately crumbles and agrees he's not worthy
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation moment where Craig's anxiety becomes contagious. Bobby's instant defeat shows how Craig's catastrophic thinking can destroy anyone's confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: You're right. I'm not worthy. I'm not worthy!

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: That's right, you're not.

jokes.text: Alex, I stink.
jokes.setup: Bobby's confidence has been completely destroyed
jokes.punchline: He admits total self-doubt about his abilities
jokes.explanation: Good continuation showing Craig's negative influence spreading. Bobby's complete breakdown creates sympathy and shows how anxiety can be transmitted.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I can't do anything right. I'm a complete failure as a driver, as an actor, as a person. I don't know why anyone even bothers talking to me.

jokes.text: I feel kind of stupid.
jokes.setup: Craig has just collapsed in panic and is slowly recovering
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement about his complete breakdown
jokes.explanation: Perfect understatement that shows Craig's self-awareness despite his condition. 'Stupid' is a mild word for what just happened, creating gentle humor.

dialogue.speaker: Craig
dialogue.line: I'm fine. Just a little stressed.

jokes.text: Good night, Mr. Walters!
jokes.setup: Episode ends with routine taxi dispatch business
jokes.punchline: Cheerful farewell to presumably another eccentric regular customer
jokes.explanation: Standard Taxi ending showing normal business continuing after emotional episodes. Provides comfort through routine but isn't particularly funny on its own.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Take care of yourself, and don't do anything I wouldn't do.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: That doesn't leave much out, does it?

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Nope!

tmdb_overview: Elaine makes it her mission to try to cure a talented and friendly but troubled artist of his severe agoraphobia (fear of public places). Alex (possibly jealous of Elaine spending time with another man) advises Elaine to leave the treatment to a professional, but she ignores his advice and eventually brings the artist to the garage. Predictably, the artist is scared to leave the cab but while Alex and Elaine bicker about her methods, the artist walks out of the cab and appears to be making progress.
insight: Forty-two jokes in twenty minutes—solid density masks middling laughs and forgettable standouts.
